chunks.text: Ladies and gentlemen, do you have doubts about the Christian faith? Are you a hundred percent certain that Christianity is true? In fact, can you be a hundred percent certain that Christianity is true? And if you do have doubts, how can you use those doubts to actually get answers and be secure in what you believe so you can be a better ambassador for others as well? Well, you know, I've been on college campuses for a little over 20 years, but our guests today and his son have been on college campuses. The main guest, the father here, has been on since the 1980s, ladies and gentlemen. You've seen him. Their website is givemeananswer. And his son, when he started to talk to his dad about doing this, he's like, Dad, I don't know if I want to go on college campuses. Well, he's been doing it for at least a decade now. They are Cliff and Stuart Kneckley, and here they are, ladies and gentlemen, all the way enough with the audience, all the way from New Canaan, Connecticut. And the new book, by the way, they've put together. I have it in Kindle. It's called Demolishing Doubt, Demolishing Doubt, Discover How Your Deepest Questions Can Lead to Life-Giving Faith. It's always great to see Cliff and Stuart. They do such great work out there. They've, as I say, Cliff, you've been on college campuses since, what, 1981? How far back do we go? 1980. 1980. Now, I'm sitting near Charlotte, North Carolina right now, and you went on a basketball scholarship. You inspired Steph Curry to go to Davidson, I'm sure, because you were there. I was not on a basketball scholarship. I was a walk-on, and I had to compete for every game. Thank you, though, Frank. All right. All right. So you went to Davidson, which is like an hour north of where I'm sitting right now, and then you ran into a man by the name of Layton Ford, who was somehow related to Billy Graham. Explain that connection. Billy Graham's brother-in-law is Layton Ford. Layton was married to Jeannie, Billy's sister. Okay. But he was a minister himself, wasn't he? Yes, correct. Evangelist on the Billy Graham team. And how did you meet him from Davidson College? How did that happen? Mom and Dad had worked with Billy Graham on the 1954-6 crusade in New York City. Okay. And so when we went down there, Mom and Dad introduced me to Layton and Jean. All right. And so when you came to Davidson, he was in the area already, and was he your mentor? Like, what happened? Well, they invited me over one Sunday, and at the end of the Sunday, they said, we'll see you hopefully next week. And then the next Sunday, they said, we'll hopefully see you next week. And they became like a family away from home for me while I was at Davidson College, and I spent the weekends with them. You spent the weekends with Layton Ford, a great evangelist and brother-in-law of Billy Graham. And then how did you go from Davidson to going to college campuses, out on the quad, answering difficult questions? Well, Layton encouraged me to go to Gordon-Conwell Theological Seminary, so I did for three years, got my Master of Divinity there. And then I went through the door that God opened to get to the most non-Christians, which was InterVarsity Christian Fellowship, doing evangelism on the Boston College campuses. So that's where I started, but I got real frustrated because the only people who came to the meetings that I spoke at in college dorm lounges were Christians. And so I decided to leave InterVarsity because I was not going to spend my life telling Christians how to accept Christ. When Layton, who had been speaking at the University of Arizona, saw a hellfire brimstone preacher stand up and tell all the students they were going to hell in a handcart, and he said, Cliff, why don't you go out and present both the love and the truth of Christ? So Brother, 46 years ago on the beaches of Fort Lauderdale, Florida, when everybody's out there sunbathing during spring break, InterVarsity had a beach evangelism project. I stood up and began to preach, and students huffed off their towels and started interrupting and firing questions at me. And so I scrapped my outline and started answering their questions. And after about an hour, I said, my voice is tired. I'll see you all later. And I went back to the hotel where the InterVarsity staff and students were, and they said, hey, Cliff, would you try this on our campus? And I did, and haven't stopped. And how did you learn all the apologetic answers? Was it by necessity or did you take classes in apologetics? No, it was by necessity. Beginning in about middle school, we lived in a very secular part of the nation, Connecticut, and people challenged my faith on a regular basis. And then on in Davidson College, people challenged my faith regularly. And then on into Gordon-Conwell Seminary, I would go down to the inner city of Boston and work among the homeless and work with guys on vacation down there trying to hook up with a prostitute. And so I began to, you know, have to think through the issues. And then I went on staff with InterVarsity. I was asked a lot of questions. I didn't have the foggiest answer, foggiest idea how to answer. And so I went to the InterVarsity staff worker and said, guys, I can't answer that question. How would you answer it? And slowly I built a pool of knowledge up from these InterVarsity staff workers, InterVarsity Press books and other books like your great book on Can't Have Enough Faith to Be an Atheist, and built up a pool of answers to those questions. Now, Stuart, what was it like growing up in this family? Your dad was on the road a lot, get in the car, go to a university, could be hours and hours away. What was it like growing up in that kind of home? Yeah, the two top reasons so many kids leave the faith of pastors, of pastor's kids are one, because the pastor's always gone, or they're hanging out with just the other kids. And then two, they're really hypocritical and they preach one thing and live another. And so I was fortunate enough to have a dad who strongly avoided both of those. I think probably a lot of the influence came from Layton and the Billy Graham friends. And so that level of consistency of character, sadly, we see so many spiritual leaders fall in this day and age. And I think a lot of that has probably to do with a unhealthy or just lack of a devotional life. And so I got to grow up seeing my mom really healthy devotional life. I'm reading a ton of the word. He would study until like 9:30 at night and then put the books away and he'd go out underneath the stars and pray and just have memorized Scripture for two hours straight. And so the discipline there is one of my other spiritual hero, Tim Keller, also really put in place. So seeing that I was really lucky to have kind of those different forms of dynamics of spirituality. And that helps a lot. And what made it that you wanted to go to college campuses? Because in the book, you were kind of reluctant to go do that. What was it that you said, well, I'll go with dad and maybe I could do some of this too? Yeah, I think when we started in a little bit of a youth group out of their basement, and like he mentioned, it's such a secular town in Connecticut, tremendously wealthy. And I see these kids who are all about all kinds of drugs and other stuff and just confused with their gender and that sort of thing. All of a sudden they would come, they would join and then ask these tough questions. And that's the only thing that would lead them actually to really go home or to any type of church related thing. And so I saw that there was a real evangelic. I grew a real passion for that. And then I accepted a thing 24/7 on Nova Scotia. Randomly, I was not a reader. Let's just put it that way. But randomly, I would remember those jars. I started to really like apologetics, theology, church history books. And I would read the entire 60 hours. And it was especially one summer. So I think that was the second thing that really juiced me up. And then I saw, wow, this is a real connection here to our church and being a pastor. So why would I be missing a great opportunity to spread the gospel in general and also to minister to our church and hang out if I didn't do this? Yeah, for those who don't know, Pastor Cliff and Pastor Stuart are pastors of Grace Community Church in New Canaan, Connecticut. So they're pastors first before they're campus folks. They're preaching every Sunday or teaching every Sunday. And then they go out to campuses and start with a very short, maybe talk on something, and then it's Q&A the rest of the way. And their YouTube channel and Instagram page has exploded. Stuart, you're probably most responsible for that, making sure that got up and going. But Cliff, what's changed over the past four decades in terms of questions that are being asked on a college campus? How's it changed from say the 80s to now where we are in 2026? The questions have not changed that much, but the emotional aura has. People are more emotionally fragile today than they were in the 80s and 90s. So I've had to be more sensitive
